unit.subject: English
unit.unitOverview: Welcome to Unit 2 of your Year 8 English course. In this unit, you will explore the fascinating world of design, packaging, and sustainability through the lens of a powerful story called "Signal in the Static." This story follows two courageous Somali students, Samira and Ibrahim, who run a youth radio station in Hargeisa and use their platform to investigate a secret land deal that threatens community gardens. Their journey will show you how design thinking, clear communication, and a commitment to truth can make a real difference in your community.
unit.learningPath: Preview the goals and vocabulary. Read and annotate the source texts. Compare evidence, viewpoint and source reliability. Apply the grammar focus. Draft and revise a cohesive multi-paragraph response. Present, defend, reflect and complete the quiz.

visual.alt: Learners evaluating design, packaging and sustainability

readings.title: Design, Packaging, and Sustainability: source text 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Welcome to Unit 2 of your Year 8 English course. In this unit, you will explore the fascinating world of design, packaging, and sustainability through the lens of a powerful story called "Signal in the Static." This story follows two courageous Somali students, Samira and Ibrahim, who run a youth radio station in Hargeisa and use their platform to investigate a secret land deal that threatens community gardens. Their journey will show you how design thinking, clear communication, and a commitment to truth can make a real difference in your community.
Throughout this unit, you will strengthen your English skills across all four domains: reading, writing, speaking, and listening. You will learn new vocabulary that helps you express complex ideas about investigation, emotion, and public policy. You will study partitive expressions as your primary grammar focus, along with zero and first conditionals, relative clauses, comparatives and superlatives, and passive voice. Each of these grammar tools will help you communicate with greater precision and confidence.
The theme of design, packaging, and sustainability connects to the story in a meaningful way. Just as designers must think carefully about how they present information and products to the public, Samira and Ibrahim must design their radio broadcasts to be clear, honest, and impactful. Just as sustainable packaging avoids waste and protects the environment, sustainable journalism avoids sensationalism and protects the truth. You will see how the principles of good design apply not only to physical products but also to the way we communicate ideas and hold institutions accountable.
This unit is designed for self-paced learning, which means you can work through the material at your own speed. You have access to a 24/7 AI tutor who can help you whenever you are stuck, and you will also meet with your teacher twice a week for guided sessions. Take your time with each section, revisit anything that feels difficult, and remember that learning is a journey, not a race.
By the end of this unit, you will be able to:
Read and analyse a narrative text, identifying themes of truth, bias, manipulation, and civic responsibility.
Use partitive expressions correctly and confidently in both speaking and writing (e.g., a piece of evidence, a surge of indignation, a multitude of voices).
Form and use zero and first conditionals to express general truths and real future possibilities.
Construct relative clauses with who, that, and which to add detail and clarity to your sentences.
Compare and contrast ideas using comparative and superlative forms of adjectives.
Recognise and produce passive voice sentences, understanding when and why writers choose passive over active constructions.
Ask formal questions beginning with prepositions (e.g., From which perspective was the story told?).
Define, spell, and use all 32 vocabulary words from this unit in context.
Design and deliver a short spoken presentation on a topic related to youth involvement in public policy.
Write a persuasive paragraph or short essay using the grammar and vocabulary from this unit.
Evaluate sources and identify bias in media narratives.
Reflect on your own learning using self-assessment checklists and study journals.
Before you dive into the story and grammar lessons, take some time to preview the key vocabulary for this unit. These words appear throughout "Signal in the Static" and will also be used in your grammar exercises, writing tasks, and speaking activities. Familiarising yourself with these words now will make the rest of the unit much easier to navigate.
The nouns in this unit help you talk about stories, ideas, conflicts, and the natural world.
narrative — a story or description of events that tells what happened
synopsis — a short summary of a story or text that gives the main points
argument — a disagreement or discussion where people express different opinions
analysis — the process of studying something carefully to understand it better
multitude — a large number of people or things

readings.title: Design, Packaging, and Sustainability: source text 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
restitution — the act of giving back something that was lost or taken away
hindrance — something that makes it difficult for you to do something
tendril — a thin, curly part of a plant that helps it climb or hold onto something
The verbs in this unit describe strong actions and emotions. Many of them express feelings, strategies, and consequences.
exult — to feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory
loathe — to feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone
usurp — to take control of something without the right to do so
feign — to pretend to have something or to be something that you are not
intervene — to come between people or groups to help settle a problem
obstruct — to block something so that it cannot move or be seen
avert — to prevent something bad from happening
inundate — to flood or overwhelm with a large amount of something
flourish — to grow or develop in a healthy or strong way
decipher — to figure out or understand something difficult to read or understand
rebuke — to speak to someone in a way that shows you are angry because they did something wrong
quell — to stop something, especially a feeling or a situation, from becoming worse
The adjectives in this unit describe people, situations, and qualities. They will help you write more vividly and speak more precisely.
adept — very skilled or good at something
impassive — not showing any emotion or feeling; calm and controlled
sociable — someone who likes to talk and spend time with other people
implicit — suggested or understood without being directly stated
exhilarating — very exciting and happy
pertinent — related to the topic or important for understanding something
erratic — not regular or consistent; unpredictable
luminous — giving off light; bright or shining
oblique — not straight or direct; slanted or angled
monotonous — boring because it is always the same and never changes
Write each new word on a flashcard with the definition on one side and an example sentence on the other.
Try to use at least three new vocabulary words in your conversations each day.
Group the words by part of speech (nouns, verbs, adjectives) to help you remember them.
This unit covers several important grammar topics that will help you express yourself more clearly and precisely. Your primary grammar focus is on partitive expressions, but you will also practise zero and first conditionals, relative clauses, comparatives and superlatives, passive voice, and questions beginning with prepositions. Let us begin with an overview of each topic.
Partitive Expressions — Your Primary Focus
Partitive expressions help you talk about a portion or part of something. Instead of saying "I want cake," you can say "I want a piece of cake." Instead of saying "She felt happiness," you can say "She felt a surge of happiness." These expressions make your language more specific and more natural.
Here is how partitive expressions work:
Choose the right partitive word. Common ones include: a piece of, a cup of, a slice of, a bit of, a lot of, a surge of, a multitude of, a handful of, a drop of.
Combine them. Put the partitive word before the noun: a piece of cake, a glass of water, a surge of indignation, a multitude of calls.
Use them in sentences. For example: "There was a surge of excitement when Samira went on air." Or: "A multitude of listeners called the station."
Why do partitive expressions matter for this unit? In the story "Signal in the Static," Samira and Ibrahim deal with large quantities of information, strong emotions, and complex situations. Partitive expressions help the author describe these things precisely. When you read phrases like "a surge of indignation" or "a multitude of calls," you are seeing partitive expressions in action. Learning to use them will make your own writing more vivid and more mature.

readings.title: Signal in the Static, part 1
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
Genre: Action drama / political mystery
Characters: Samira Adam (student journalist), Ibrahim (her co-host and editor), Mr. Adam (deputy mayor's aide), Sami (fellow student)
Themes: Truth, manipulation, power, civic responsibility, and the courage to ask questions
Moral: Do not follow every voice that shouts — decipher the truth for yourself.
As you read, look for the vocabulary words from Unit 2.
They appear naturally throughout the story.
When you find one, pause and check whether you can define it from context.
Samira adjusted her headphones and leaned into the mic.
"This is Horizon Youth Radio — news and truth from Hargeisa's students, for the city's future. I'm Samira Adam. Let's begin."
Behind the glass, Ibrahim gave her a thumbs-up. The red "LIVE" light glowed.
They had been running the school radio station for almost a year now. What began as a project in journalism class had grown into a respected youth platform. They had covered water shortages, exam protests, and even a segment about restitution for families displaced by flooding.
Now, something new was unfolding — and it was bigger than anything they had faced.
It began with a message slipped under the radio room door. No name. No signature.
"They're lying. What you're hearing about the city gardens? All fake. There's more beneath the soil than plants."
Ibrahim thought it was a joke. "Someone trying to be dramatic."
Samira, however, did not dismiss it. The handwriting was neat. The language was not emotional. In fact, it was calculated. Oblique, even. Suggestive, but not direct.
"I want to know more," she said.
Later that week, a new narrative emerged online: photos of government trucks near the northern edge of the public gardens. Some claimed the land was being sold to foreign developers. Others said it was just "renovation."
"New irrigation pipelines. Community gardens to flourish."
But no plans had been released. No engineer names. No contractors. Just noise.
Samira and Ibrahim began to investigate. They interviewed gardeners, market sellers, even a retired city planner. Most had not heard anything official.
One vendor near the gardens whispered, "They say a man from Djibouti came with contracts. Big money. But the families living nearby were told nothing."
Samira felt a surge of indignation. "Why wouldn't they announce it?"
"Because," the woman said, "some would fight back."
At school, Samira managed to arrange an interview with the deputy mayor's aide, a man named Mr. Adam. He was tall, calm, and wore an impassive expression throughout the discussion.
"So the city plans are public, yes?" Samira asked.
"They're being updated. The full report will be available next month."
Ibrahim glanced at Samira. A delay. Convenient.
"Some say nearby families are being pressured to move. Quietly."
"That is incorrect," Adam said smoothly. "Those are baseless rumours."
"But who started the rumours?" Samira pressed.
Adam's expression did not change. "That's not pertinent."
Back at school, the Horizon Debate Club hosted a special event:
"Should Youth Intervene in Public Policy?"
Samira presented her argument: "If we don't ask questions, adults will write the future without us. We must intervene, investigate, and demand clarity."
A student named Sami disagreed. "We aren't experts. If we get involved in everything, we'll only create confusion. Youth action can be a hindrance, not help."
Ibrahim spoke next. "But what if we're the only ones who can spot what adults miss?"
Samira concluded with power: "Sometimes we must quell our fear to challenge authority. Especially when silence is the goal."
That night, Ibrahim was editing the next episode when his computer glitched. The files on the gardens had vanished. Just static remained.
"Someone accessed the system," he told Sarah, shocked. "They wiped it."
She stared at the blank timeline on the screen. "That's not a mistake. That's a warning."
"Stop pushing. You're kids. Don't make this worse. Not everything is your business."
Samira's throat tightened. "What if we keep going?"

readings.title: Signal in the Static, part 2
readings.setting: East African and familiar learner contexts
readings.passageScript:
"Then you'll forfeit more than files."
Ibrahim was shaken. "This is serious. If we keep digging, we could get in trouble."
"But if we stop, we let them usurp the truth."
Samira looked around their tiny studio. She loved this room — the old microphones, the battered table, even the wall where vines climbed from a pot and sent out delicate tendrils toward the light.
The moment was quiet. Monotonous, almost. But beneath it pulsed a choice.
"I say we go live tomorrow. Not with conclusions. Just with questions."
The next day, they went on air.
"Today's episode is different," Samira began. "We won't tell you what's true. We'll tell you what we found — and let you decide."
They played interviews, quoted vendors, and described the missing documents.
They even aired a segment titled "From which perspective is the garden story being told?" — a lesson in bias and source-checking.
"We're not accusing anyone," Ibrahim said. "But we are asking why so many answers have gone dark."
As they wrapped up, the studio line lit up. Call after call.
"I'm a teacher," one voice said. "I've heard the same rumours. Thank you for speaking up."
Another said, "I work at city hall. You're not wrong."
Samira exulted — silently. No cheering. Just a strong breath. A moment of pride.
Days later, the radio station's power cut during their morning slot. The school's admin claimed it was a generator issue.
Students began receiving fake emails saying the station was shutting down.
A few teachers suggested Samira and Ibrahim had gone too far.
"You're students," one said. "Stay in your lane."
Samira wrote a piece titled "Who Decides What's Pertinent?" for the school paper. It included screenshots, quotes, and passive voice to mask sources.
"The gardens were promised. The questions were raised. The files were deleted. And the truth? Still unclear."
A new document appeared in Ibrahim's locker. Anonymous again.
But this time, it was real: a scanned analysis of land permits, with signatures from the mayor's office and a private firm.
The garden land had been leased — quietly. No community vote. No consultation.
Samira stared at the papers, heart pounding.
"We need to decipher this," she said. "Every line."
They did. Then they aired a new show. No shouting. Just evidence.
They used comparatives to describe conflicting stories:
"One version is louder, another quieter. One is cleaner, the other more erratic."
"But truth," Samira ended, "is not always the most luminous. Sometimes, it hides in whispers — until someone asks the right question."
Chapter 11: A Victory, and the Price
An official investigation began. The deputy mayor resigned. Some families received formal apologies and early compensation.
They were not heroes. But they were heard.
At the school's year-end ceremony, Samira was invited to read a message.
She simply said: "Youth are not a hindrance. We are a mirror. We reflect what adults refuse to see."
The crowd stood. Applauded. Not all, but enough.
She and Ibrahim did not feel triumphant. They felt steady.
The kind of joy you do not shout about.
Look back through the story and find all the vocabulary words that were highlighted in bold.

grammar.title: Partitive Expressions
grammar.explanation:
What Are Partitive Expressions?
Partitive expressions help you talk about a portion or part of something. They make your sentences clearer and more specific. For example, instead of saying "I want cake," you can say "I want a piece of cake." Instead of saying "She felt happiness," you can say "She felt a surge of happiness." These small phrases make a big difference in how natural and precise your English sounds.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What Are Partitive Expressions?
Partitive expressions help you talk about a portion or part of something. They make your sentences clearer and more specific. For example, instead of saying "I want cake," you can say "I want a piece of cake." Instead of saying "She felt happiness," you can say "She felt a surge of happiness." These small phrases make a big difference in how natural and precise your English sounds.
Partitive expressions are especially important when you are talking about uncountable nouns — things you cannot count individually, like water, information, excitement, or sugar. Since you cannot say "two waters" or "three informations," you use partitive expressions to indicate quantity: a glass of water, a piece of information.
How to Form Partitive Expressions
Follow these steps to create partitive expressions:
Identify whether the noun is countable or uncountable. Countable nouns have singular and plural forms (apple/apples). Uncountable nouns do not (water, information, sugar).
Choose the right partitive word. The table below shows common partitive expressions and the types of nouns they go with.
Combine the partitive word with the noun. For example: a piece of + cake = a piece of cake.
Use the expression in a sentence. For example: "Can I have a piece of cake, please?"
Common Partitive Expressions
Partitive Expressions for Countable vs. Uncountable Nouns
For countable nouns, you can also use:
a few + plural noun: "I have a few friends in this class."
a couple of + plural noun: "She bought a couple of books."
several + plural noun: "There are several reasons for this decision."
For uncountable nouns, use:
a little + uncountable noun: "Add a little sugar to the tea."
a bit of + uncountable noun: "I need a bit of advice."
a great deal of + uncountable noun: "There is a great deal of information to process."
Common Mistakes with Partitive Expressions
Mistake 1: Using the wrong partitive expression for the noun type.
Incorrect: "a piece of water" — you cannot cut water into pieces!
Correct: "a glass of water" or "a drop of water"
Mistake 2: Forgetting to include the partitive word altogether.
Incorrect: "I want cake." (too general)
Correct: "I want a piece of cake." (specific and natural)
Mistake 3: Using "a lot of" when a more specific expression would be better.
Vague: "I felt a lot of excitement."
Better: "I felt a surge of excitement."
Memory Tip
💡 Quick Tip
Think of partitive expressions as sharing words.
They help you tell others exactly what part you want from a whole.
If you can count it, use a few or a couple of.
If you cannot count it, use a bit of or a little.
Practice Exercises — Partitive Expressions
Exercise 1: Fill in the Blanks
Complete each sentence with the correct partitive expression.
Can I have __________ (a piece of / a few) cake?
Pour me __________ (a cup of / a couple of) tea, please.
I need __________ (a little / a bunch) help with my homework.
She wants __________ (a slice of / a drop of) pizza.
There is __________ (a lot of / a few) excitement in the room!
Only __________ (a handful of / a glass of) students passed the difficult exam.
After the announcement, there was __________ (a surge of / a slice of) indignation among the community.
He ate __________ (a bit of / a multitude of) bread for breakfast.
Answers:
a piece of
a cup of
a little
a slice of
a lot of
a handful of
a surge of
a bit of
Exercise 2: Write Your Own Sentences
Write a sentence using each of the following partitive expressions. Make your sentences interesting and relevant to your own life.
a surge of __________
a multitude of __________
a handful of __________
a piece of __________
a bit of __________
a drop of __________

grammar.title: Zero and First Conditionals
grammar.explanation:
What Are Conditionals?
Conditionals are sentences that describe what happens (or what will happen) if a certain condition is met. English has several types of conditionals, but in this unit you will focus on two: the zero conditional and the first conditional.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What Are Conditionals?
Conditionals are sentences that describe what happens (or what will happen) if a certain condition is met. English has several types of conditionals, but in this unit you will focus on two: the zero conditional and the first conditional.
Zero Conditional — Facts and General Truths
The zero conditional describes things that are always true. It is used for scientific facts, general truths, and reliable cause-and-effect relationships.
Structure: If + present simple, present simple
Examples:
If you heat ice, it melts.
If you mix red and blue, you get purple.
If it rains, the grass gets wet.
If plants do not get water, they die.
You can also use when instead of if in zero conditional sentences, because the result always happens: "When you heat ice, it melts."
First Conditional — Real Future Possibilities
The first conditional describes things that are possible or likely to happen in the future. It is used when you believe there is a real chance that the condition will be fulfilled.
Structure: If + present simple, will + base verb
Examples:
If I study hard, I will pass the test.
If it rains tomorrow, I will bring an umbrella.
If we intervene now, we will avert disaster.
If you feign calm, they will push harder.
Variations: You can use might or can instead of will when the result is less certain:
If I finish my homework, I might play outside.
If you ask politely, she can help you.
Common Mistakes with Conditionals
Mistake 1: Using the wrong tense in the if-clause.
Incorrect: "If you will study hard, you will pass."
Correct: "If you study hard, you will pass." (Present simple in the if-clause, not will.)
Mistake 2: Confusing zero and first conditionals.
Zero: "If you heat water, it boils." (Always true — a fact.)
First: "If you heat the water, I will make tea." (A specific future possibility.)
Practice Exercises — Conditionals
Exercise 1: Identify the Type
Read each sentence and write whether it is a zero conditional or a first conditional.
If you drop a ball, it falls. __________
If it snows tomorrow, we will build a snowman. __________
If you press the button, the door opens. __________
If Samira investigates the story, she will uncover the truth. __________
If water reaches 100 degrees, it boils. __________
Answers: 1. Zero  2. First  3. Zero  4. First  5. Zero
Exercise 2: Complete the Sentences
Fill in the blanks with the correct verb forms.
If you __________ (study) every day, you __________ (improve) your English. (First)
If water __________ (freeze), it __________ (turn) to ice. (Zero)
If we __________ (leave) now, we __________ (arrive) on time. (First)
If you __________ (not water) plants, they __________ (die). (Zero)
If she __________ (practise) speaking, she __________ (become) more confident. (First)
Answers:
study, will improve
freezes, turns
leave, will arrive
do not water, die
practises, will become

grammar.title: Relative Clauses
grammar.explanation:
What Are Relative Clauses?
Relative clauses are parts of a sentence that give you extra information about a noun. They begin with relative pronouns: who (for people), that (for things or people), and which (for things). Relative clauses make your sentences more detailed and interesting.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What Are Relative Clauses?
Relative clauses are parts of a sentence that give you extra information about a noun. They begin with relative pronouns: who (for people), that (for things or people), and which (for things). Relative clauses make your sentences more detailed and interesting.
How to Form Relative Clauses
Identify the noun you want to give more information about.
Choose the correct relative pronoun: who (people), that (things/people), which (things).
Add the extra information after the relative pronoun.
Check punctuation: defining clauses have no commas; non-defining clauses use commas.
Defining vs. Non-Defining Relative Clauses
Defining clauses give essential information. Without them, the sentence does not make complete sense. They do not use commas:
"The student who won the debate is in my class." (Which student? The one who won.)
"The document that contained the permits was anonymous." (Which document? The one with permits.)
Non-defining clauses give extra, non-essential information. The sentence still makes sense without them. They use commas:
"Samira, who runs the radio station, is very brave." (We already know which Samira.)
"The radio station, which was started as a school project, became very popular." (Extra detail.)
Common Mistakes with Relative Clauses
Mistake 1: Using which for people.
Incorrect: "The teacher which helped us is very kind."
Correct: "The teacher who helped us is very kind."
Mistake 2: Forgetting commas in non-defining clauses.
Incorrect: "My brother who enjoys maths is studying for his test." (This implies you have multiple brothers.)
Correct: "My brother, who enjoys maths, is studying for his test." (Extra info about your only brother.)
Practice Exercises — Relative Clauses
Exercise 1: Choose the Correct Pronoun
Fill in each blank with who, that, or which.
The girl __________ won the race is my friend.
I have a book __________ is very interesting.
The teacher __________ helps us is very kind.
The flowers, __________ are blooming, smell wonderful.
The argument __________ he presented was flawed.
The plant, __________ tendrils flourished in the light, grew taller every day.
Answers: 1. who  2. that  3. who  4. which  5. that  6. whose
Exercise 2: Combine the Sentences
Combine each pair of sentences into one sentence using a relative clause.
Ibrahim edits audio. Ibrahim is Samira's partner at the radio station.
The message was anonymous. The message started the investigation.
Mr. Adam gave evasive answers. Mr. Adam works for the deputy mayor.
The gardens are threatened. The gardens are in the northern part of the city.
Answers:
Ibrahim, who is Samira's partner at the radio station, edits audio.
The message that started the investigation was anonymous.
Mr. Adam, who works for the deputy mayor, gave evasive answers.
The gardens that are in the northern part of the city are threatened.

grammar.title: Questions Beginning with Prepositions
grammar.explanation:
What Are Prepositional Questions?
In formal English, you can place a preposition at the beginning of a question rather than at the end. This makes the question sound more polished and academic.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What Are Prepositional Questions?
In formal English, you can place a preposition at the beginning of a question rather than at the end. This makes the question sound more polished and academic.
Informal: Which perspective was the story told from?
Formal: From which perspective was the story told?
How to Form Prepositional Questions
Identify the preposition in the informal question (from, with, to, about, for, etc.).
Move the preposition to the beginning of the question.
Follow it with the question word (which, whom, what).
Complete the sentence with the correct word order.
Examples
Practice Exercise — Rewrite Formally
Rewrite each question with the preposition at the beginning.
Which teacher do you like learning from?
Which country did this package come from?
Which friend are you going to the library with?
Which issue is the article about?
Which person did you address the letter to?
Answers:
From which teacher do you like learning?
From which country did this package come?
With which friend are you going to the library?
About which issue is the article?
To which person did you address the letter?

grammar.title: Comparatives and Superlatives
grammar.explanation:
What Are Comparatives and Superlatives?
Comparatives are used to compare two things. Superlatives are used to describe the highest or lowest degree among three or more things.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What Are Comparatives and Superlatives?
Comparatives are used to compare two things. Superlatives are used to describe the highest or lowest degree among three or more things.
How to Form Comparatives
The rule depends on the length of the adjective:
How to Form Superlatives
Irregular Comparatives and Superlatives
Using Comparatives and Superlatives from the Story
In "Signal in the Static," Samira uses comparatives to describe conflicting versions of events:
"One version is louder, another quieter."
"One is cleaner, the other more erratic."
"Truth is not always the most luminous."
Practice Exercises — Comparatives and Superlatives
Exercise 1: Write the Correct Form
My brother is __________ (funny) than my sister.
Today is the __________ (cold) day of the year.
This book is __________ (interesting) than that one.
She is the __________ (good) student in the class.
The second film was __________ (bad) than the first.
Of all the planets, Jupiter is the __________ (large).
Answers: 1. funnier  2. coldest  3. more interesting  4. best  5. worse  6. largest

grammar.title: Passive Voice
grammar.explanation:
What Is the Passive Voice?
The passive voice shifts the focus of a sentence from who did the action to what was done. It is useful when the doer is unknown, unimportant, or when you want to emphasise the action itself.
grammar.ruleAndExamples:
What Is the Passive Voice?
The passive voice shifts the focus of a sentence from who did the action to what was done. It is useful when the doer is unknown, unimportant, or when you want to emphasise the action itself.
Active: The teacher read the book.
Passive: The book was read by the teacher.
How to Form the Passive Voice
Find the object of the active sentence. (What receives the action?)
Make the object the new subject of the passive sentence.
Use the correct form of "to be" (was/were for past tense, is/are for present tense).
Add the past participle of the main verb.
Optionally add "by" + the doer if it is important to know who did it.
Passive Voice Tense Table
Passive Voice in the Story
Samira deliberately uses passive voice in her newspaper article to create a powerful effect:
"The gardens were promised."
"The questions were raised."
"The files were deleted."
"The permits were signed."
Notice how the passive voice creates mystery — we do not know who promised, raised, deleted, or signed. This is a strategic writing choice that you can use in your own work.
Common Mistakes with Passive Voice
Mistake 1: Using the wrong form of "to be."
Incorrect: "The book is read yesterday." (Past action needs past tense.)
Correct: "The book was read yesterday."
Mistake 2: Forgetting to use the past participle.
Incorrect: "The letter was wrote by my friend."
Correct: "The letter was written by my friend."
Practice Exercises — Passive Voice
Exercise 1: Rewrite in Passive Voice
The chef baked the cake.
Someone stole my bicycle.
The students completed the project.
The wind destroyed the old building.
Samira wrote the article.
Answers:
The cake was baked by the chef.
My bicycle was stolen.
The project was completed by the students.
The old building was destroyed by the wind.
The article was written by Samira.
Exercise 2: Active or Passive?
Identify whether each sentence is active or passive.
The message was deciphered by the students.
Ibrahim edited the audio files.
The land permits were signed by the mayor.
Samira broadcast the truth to the city.
The investigation was launched after the broadcast.
Answers: 1. Passive  2. Active  3. Passive  4. Active  5. Passive
📖 Grammar Summary
Partitive Expressions: a piece of, a surge of, a multitude of, etc.
Zero Conditional: If + present simple, present simple (facts)
First Conditional: If + present simple, will + base verb (real possibilities)
Relative Clauses: who (people), that/which (things), with or without commas
Comparatives: -er or more + adjective (two things)
Superlatives: -est or most + adjective (three or more things)

writing.title: Writing 1: Persuasive Paragraph
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a persuasive paragraph (100 to 150 words) arguing either for or against the following statement: "Young people should be allowed to investigate and report on public policy issues." Your paragraph must include:
A clear topic sentence that states your position
At least two partitive expressions (e.g., a piece of evidence, a multitude of reasons)
At least one conditional sentence (zero or first conditional)
At least one relative clause
A strong concluding sentence that reinforces your argument
writing.modelText: A strong Grade 8 response sustains a line of thought across cohesive paragraphs, integrates precise vocabulary and evidence, evaluates sources and shapes language for audience and purpose.
writing.sentenceStarter: The evidence supports the view that...
writing.successCriteria: I sustained a clear line of thought; shaped writing for audience and purpose; integrated precise vocabulary and attributed evidence; compared viewpoints and source reliability; used cohesive devices and varied structures; revised content and accuracy
writing.support: Plan a thesis or controlling idea, paragraph sequence, evidence and source notes before drafting.
writing.extension: Synthesize three sources, qualify claims, address and rebut a counterargument, and refine style for deliberate effect.

writing.title: Writing 2: News Report in Passive Voice
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Imagine you are a journalist reporting on the events in "Signal in the Static." Write a short news report (80 to 120 words) using the passive voice for at least five sentences. Your report should cover the key events: the anonymous message, the investigation, the deleted files, the broadcast, and the outcome. Here is an example to get you started:
"An anonymous message was discovered at Horizon Youth Radio. An investigation was launched by two students, Samira and Ibrahim..."

writing.title: Writing 3: Comparative Essay
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Write a short essay (150 to 200 words) comparing two characters from the story. You might compare Samira and Ibrahim, Samira and Sami, or Samira and Mr. Adam. Use comparatives and superlatives throughout your essay. Consider these questions:
Who is more courageous, and why?
Who is more cautious, and why?
Who has the strongest argument in the debate scene?
Who is the most important character in the story, and what makes them so?

writing.title: Writing 4: Reflective Journal Entry

What were your options?
How did you feel?
What did you decide, and what happened as a result?

writing.title: Writing 5:
writing.promptAndInstructions:
Before you submit any writing task, use this checklist to review your work:
Does your writing have a clear beginning, middle, and end?
Have you used the required grammar structures (partitive expressions, conditionals, relative clauses, comparatives, passive voice)?
Have you included the required number of vocabulary words?
Is your spelling correct? Pay special attention to the vocabulary words.
Is your punctuation correct? Check commas in relative clauses and conditional sentences.
Does your writing make sense? Read it aloud to check for clarity.
Have you proofread for any repeated words or awkward sentences?

activities.title: Activities 1: Partitive Expressions — Fill in the Blanks
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Complete each sentence with the correct partitive expression from the box below.
Options: a piece of | a surge of | a multitude of | a bit of | a glass of | a handful of | a drop of | a lot of
Samira felt __________ indignation when she learned about the secret deal.
Can I have __________ water, please? I am very thirsty.
After the broadcast, __________ listeners called the station to share their stories.
Ibrahim needed __________ help fixing the computer after the files were deleted.
The detective found __________ evidence at the scene of the crime.
There was only __________ milk left in the fridge, so we could not make tea.
She picked up __________ almonds from the bowl and ate them quickly.
The students had __________ questions after the presentation, so the teacher stayed late.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 2: Zero and First Conditionals — Match and Complete
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Part A: Match the beginnings with the correct endings to form conditional sentences.
If you heat ice, __________. (a) I will bring an umbrella.
If it rains tomorrow, __________. (b) it melts.
If Samira investigates the story, __________. (c) you get green.
If you mix blue and yellow, __________. (d) she will uncover the truth.
Part B: Complete each sentence with the correct verb form.
If you __________ (study) every day, you __________ (improve) your English quickly. (First conditional)
If water __________ (reach) 100 degrees, it __________ (boil). (Zero conditional)
If we __________ (intervene) now, we __________ (avert) the problem. (First conditional)
If you __________ (not eat) breakfast, you __________ (feel) tired by midmorning. (Zero conditional)
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 3: Relative Clauses — Combine the Sentences
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Combine each pair of sentences into one sentence using a relative clause with who, that, or which.
Samira runs a radio station. Samira is a student at the school.
The document contained land permits. The document appeared in Ibrahim's locker.
Mr. Adam wore an impassive expression. Mr. Adam is the deputy mayor's aide.
The broadcast received many calls. The broadcast discussed the garden story.
The vine grew towards the light. The vine had delicate tendrils.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 4: Comparatives and Superlatives — Write the Correct Form
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Write the correct comparative or superlative form of the adjective in brackets.
Samira is __________ (brave) than Ibrahim when it comes to asking difficult questions.
This was the __________ (exciting) broadcast the station had ever produced.
Mr. Adam's answers were __________ (vague) than the students expected.
Of all the characters, Samira is the __________ (determined) to find the truth.
The second version of the story was __________ (erratic) than the first.
Ibrahim is the __________ (adept) person in the school at editing audio files.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 5: Passive Voice — Rewrite the Sentences
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Rewrite each sentence in the passive voice. Remember the pattern: subject + was/were + past participle.
Someone deleted the files from the computer.
The mayor's office signed the land permits.
Samira wrote an article for the school paper.
The students produced a powerful broadcast.
An anonymous source delivered the evidence to Ibrahim.
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

activities.title: Activities 6: Questions with Prepositions — Rewrite Formally
activities.instructionsAndItems:
Rewrite each informal question in the formal style, with the preposition at the beginning.
Which perspective was the story told from?
Which student did you work with on the project?
Which school does she go to?
Which topic are you writing about?
activities.answerSummary: Accept accurate responses supported by unit vocabulary, text evidence or a clear demonstration.

quizzes.question: What does 'exult' mean?
quizzes.options: To come between two people or groups to help settle a problem or stop something bad. | To feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory. | To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not. | To take control of something, especially a position of power, without the right to do so.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory.
quizzes.explanation: After winning the game, the team began to exult and celebrate their victory.

quizzes.question: What does 'loathe' mean?
quizzes.options: To block something so that it cannot move or be seen. | To come between two people or groups to help settle a problem or stop something bad. | To feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone. | To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone.
quizzes.explanation: I loathe doing homework on weekends when the weather is beautiful.

quizzes.question: What does 'usurp' mean?
quizzes.options: To block something so that it cannot move or be seen. | To come between two people or groups to help settle a problem or stop something bad. | To prevent something bad from happening. | To take control of something, especially a position of power, without the right to do so.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To take control of something, especially a position of power, without the right to do so.
quizzes.explanation: The student tried to usurp the class president's role by making all the decisions.

quizzes.question: What does 'feign' mean?
quizzes.options: To block something so that it cannot move or be seen. | To flood or overwhelm with a large amount of something. | To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not. | To prevent something bad from happening.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not.
quizzes.explanation: She tried to feign happiness, but everyone could see she was sad.

quizzes.question: What does 'intervene' mean?
quizzes.options: To come between two people or groups to help settle a problem or stop something bad. | To flood or overwhelm with a large amount of something. | To grow or develop in a healthy or strong way. | To prevent something bad from happening.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To come between two people or groups to help settle a problem or stop something bad.
quizzes.explanation: The teacher had to intervene when the students started arguing.

quizzes.question: What does 'obstruct' mean?
quizzes.options: To block something so that it cannot move or be seen. | To figure out or understand something that is difficult to read or understand. | To flood or overwhelm with a large amount of something. | To grow or develop in a healthy or strong way.
quizzes.correctAnswer: To block something so that it cannot move or be seen.
quizzes.explanation: The fallen tree obstructed the road, making it hard for cars to pass.

To feel very happy and excited, especially because of a success or victory. After winning the game, the team began to exult and celebrate their victory.
To feel strong dislike or hatred for something or someone. I loathe doing homework on weekends when the weather is beautiful.
To take control of something, especially a position of power, without the right to do so. The student tried to usurp the class president's role by making all the decisions.
To pretend to have something or to be something that you are not. She tried to feign happiness, but everyone could see she was sad.
To come between two people or groups to help settle a problem or stop something bad. The teacher had to intervene when the students started arguing.
To block something so that it cannot move or be seen. The fallen tree obstructed the road, making it hard for cars to pass.
To prevent something bad from happening. She turned her head to avert her eyes from the bright light.
To flood or overwhelm with a large amount of something. After the heavy rain, the streets were inundated with water.
To grow or develop in a healthy or strong way. The plants in the garden flourish when they get enough sunlight and water.
To figure out or understand something that is difficult to read or understand. I had to decipher the secret message written in code.
